title: The Workshop Door
text: You begin as an apprentice in a busy workshop. Tools hang on the walls, iron parts cover the benches, and your master says inventions are changing Britain.
historyNote: Apprentices learned skilled work by training with experienced workers. Engineers helped build and improve the machines of the Industrial Revolution.
evidence: The Industrial Revolution created new skilled jobs for people who could design, build and repair machines.

choices.label: Study the drawings before touching the tools.
Start filing an iron part straight away.
Ask which invention changed textiles first.

title: The Flying Shuttle
text: Your master shows you a flying shuttle. It lets one weaver send thread across a loom much faster than before. Faster weaving creates a new problem: more thread is needed.
historyNote: John Kay's flying shuttle, invented in 1733, sped up weaving. It increased demand for spun thread.
evidence: The flying shuttle made weaving faster and encouraged further textile inventions.

choices.label: Practise explaining how the shuttle works.
Ask how workers reacted to faster machines.
Look for an invention that can spin more thread.

title: The Spinning Jenny
text: You see a spinning jenny with many spindles. One worker can spin several threads at once. The machine looks simple, but it changes the pace of textile work.
historyNote: James Hargreaves invented the spinning jenny in the 1760s. It helped increase the supply of thread for weaving.
evidence: The spinning jenny increased textile production by allowing one worker to spin many threads.

choices.label: Learn how the spindles turn together.
Ask why some spinners disliked the machine.
Design a stronger frame for the machine.

title: The Water Frame
text: A water frame turns with power from a river. It spins stronger thread than the jenny. Your master says machines like this helped move textile work into factories.
historyNote: Richard Arkwright's water frame used water power to spin strong cotton thread. It encouraged factory production near rivers.
evidence: The water frame helped shift textile production from homes to factories powered by water.

choices.label: Inspect the water wheel and gears.
Ask what happens when a factory is not near a river.
Speak to workers about factory life.

title: Steam Power
text: You stand beside a steam engine as it pumps and turns. It does not need a river. With enough coal, it can power mines, mills and transport.
historyNote: Improved steam engines, especially those linked with James Watt, helped factories and mines use power away from rivers.
evidence: Steam engines allowed industry to grow beyond places with water power.

choices.label: Learn how steam moves the piston.
Take paid work feeding coal into the engine.
Imagine using steam to move vehicles.

title: Coal and Iron
text: The ironworks glows with heat. Coal fires help make iron for machines, rails, bridges and tools. The work is skilled, hot and risky.
historyNote: Coal and iron were key resources of the Industrial Revolution. Coal supplied power and heat, while iron supplied strong materials.
evidence: Coal and iron supported machine building, steam power, factories and transport.

choices.label: Learn how iron parts are cast.
Record how smoke affects workers nearby.
Ask how heavy coal and iron are transported.

title: The Canal Wharf
text: At the canal wharf, barges carry coal, iron and cotton. A horse pulls a heavy load along the towpath. Water transport makes bulky goods cheaper to move.
historyNote: Canals helped move heavy goods before railways became common. They linked mines, factories, ports and towns.
evidence: Canals lowered transport costs and helped connect industrial places.

choices.label: Map the route from mine to factory.
Work out how to move goods faster.
Ask canal workers if they fear railways.

title: Machines and Workers
text: Some workers admire the new machines. Others worry that machines will lower wages or replace skilled work. You realise inventions affect people as well as production.
historyNote: Industrial inventions increased output, but they could also disrupt older jobs and create difficult factory conditions.
evidence: New machines brought progress for industry but uncertainty for many workers.

choices.label: Design machines that are safer to use.
Focus only on making machines faster.
Listen to workers before changing a machine.

title: Changing Transport
text: Roads, canals and steam power are changing how goods move. A factory owner wants faster transport from coalfields to mills and ports.
historyNote: Better transport helped industrialisation by moving raw materials and finished goods more quickly and cheaply.
evidence: Transport improvements helped factories obtain materials and sell goods in wider markets.

choices.label: Improve canal loading equipment.
Study plans for a steam railway.
Ask who benefits and who loses from faster transport.

title: Rails and Locomotives
text: A locomotive stands on iron rails. It can move goods and passengers faster than horses or canals. People cheer, but some worry about noise, accidents and lost jobs.
historyNote: Railways became a major transport innovation. They used steam engines, coal and iron to connect cities, ports, mines and factories.
evidence: Railways showed how inventions, coal, iron and transport worked together to change Britain.
